entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: Yeah, yeah. You'll discover I'm a talker. Excuse me.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: Please introduce yourself and describe your organization or initiative and what you do.

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: Oh, shucks, let me get this out of my system. My name's Luby Reich. I have. I'm a sole proprietor of Making Course, which is. No, I'm starting all over again. My name is Luby Rasch. I've been working as Making course since about 2010, and under that brand I've been exploring the indigenous foods of a Cape touristic region, which is more or less the Western Cape, to discover what are our edibles and how to use them. Then I also have founded the local Wild Food Hub. I did that in about 2016 or so. And since 2022, I've mostly based the project work that I do under that umbrella at the Sustainability Institute for a number of reasons. Firstly, because they have a really very integrated environment here for growing, for cooking with. For feeding to incoming people, as well as to the children in the school system. So it's a really wonderful sort of integrated ecosystem in which to see how forgotten foods can begin to be revived in use and cultivated.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: Ms. Leviu, what gap of problems in the Western Cape food system pushed you towards starting this initiative?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: It's evolved over the years. In the beginning, well, I very first got interested in working with indigenous foods because I was aware of how forgotten they are and how amazing they are and how long, for how long they were in use. My stepfather was an archaeologist, and so from a child I knew that the people that lived here before the colonists came ate exclusively off these landscapes. And yet none of these foods are effectively. Well, the most minuscule number of these foods are in our current food system. So, for example, bartabloomonkis you might find, and rooibos di and honeybush tea. And that's kind of it, considering that there are about 1,700 that we could actually be exploring and making use of. So for me, that reality was speaking to an erasure that I felt needs to be addressed. It's a social erasure, it's an economic erasure, it's a biodiversity erasure that in the context of climate variability and the need to heat incredible imbalances and wrongs that have taken place in this country, food is incredibly well placed to facilitate pathways of healing.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: When and how did your initiative start? Who were the founders or early role players in starting making goose?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: That was me. It was me. I'm still a sole proprietor in that initiative. I kind of spend a little bit less time there now. And it's me messing around my kitchen, really innovating with these ingredients. In terms of Local Wild, currently it's me as well. I haven't registered it as an organisation because I realised, I wrote up founding documents, but I realised that it's such early foundational work that was taking place that it was premature to formalise this in some form of initiative. I wanted to register it as a voluntary association, but I realized that there's a huge amount of work that needed to be done to stabilize the foundations of this before beginning to take on running an organisation. So that's also one of the reasons why this work is based here, because at the Sustainability Institute have been in existence for 25 years and the vision of the Local Wild Food hub aligns incredibly well with that of the Sustainability Institute. Institute and I have their institutional framework to support Local Wild until such time as the decision is or isn't to formalise it and kind of separate it off. But at this point in time, our alignment and the work being based here and projects happening here and partnerships being attracted towards here is the perfect place for Local Wild to be sitting.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: And which year would you say Making Course or like a Wild came about? Which year?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: 2010 was when I first really started with Making Course and I was selling at market and running workshops. And then I also had a sort of moment where in the sort of progression of learning about these foods and beginning to understand where, where my role sits in this was that I suddenly realized, no, it's not actually my place to develop a business. I need to be using this work to support reviving on the ground engagement in marginalised communities, in erased communities, embraced knowledge systems, to actually put this work where it needs to be happening and supported. So again, you know, if it was thrown into the wider landscape and these foods become really well known people, people who are well resourced and have land are the ones that are going to run with it and it's going to repeat the kind of appropriation in a different form, obviously from what happened in the colonist years with kind of cultural erasure and the erasure of knowledge, it would be repeating that mistake. And actually my vision is for this work to be healing, not repeating mistakes.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: Ms. Luby, who is currently involved in your team, so your team players, your volunteers, your contributors, what are their relationship and roles? Because you spoke about the Sustainability Institute as well. So we're currently involved in the team in terms of running things, in terms of the initiative.

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: So I have, I'm supported by all the kind of institutional structures of this place. So the finance team helps, the communications team supports in putting sharing information, and then the monitoring and evaluation person is supporting with when projects are being developed. The garden team is pivotally involved. We've been growing indigenous foods in the garden here for quite a few years. And we're currently also running field trials in a partnership with Kew Royal Botanical Gardens and with Gritbos Foundation. So the garden team is incredibly involved in those field trials. And then also the cooks in the nourish program. So they run the Green Cafe and they also run a feeding program for the preschool and the primary school, both Montessori schools that are run from here, that are SI schools. So foods are explored and served in the Green Cafe at events that get run here and then also in the feeding program. So you can see it's a sort of fairly well knitted. It's a really, really great place to be developing a kind of ecosystem. And then, you know, other partners like the CoE on the community of practice. So then that takes the network well beyond any projects that might be SI based. And it's beginning to attract other organizations, other academics to be involved in this kind of revitalizing of landscapes, food systems, usage of cape foods.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: What making course was this? Libby, what would you say are some of the notable achievements or milestones you.

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: Have reached this far in terms of making course from? So some of the ways in which I would measure success, and it's not done in any kind of formalized academic way, has been when I have conversations with people quite informally, like at a dinner party or whatever, about the work that I do right in the very beginning, the attention span of people hearing about this, there would be a sort of flash of interest, and then the conversation would move on quite quickly to over the years that has progressed to being that people are incredibly interested, want to learn more, are buying the books that I've written. So there's been a huge shift in interest and wanting to learn more. So chefs have begun to use these things. I've been talking at a culinary school now, I'd say probably every year for the last eight years. And even this past year, there's a student who's actually opened a restaurant that is actively using those ingredients just beyond Stellenbosch here. So there's, you know, this, this work in making these foods known. And so the two books I've written is Cape Wild Foods the Growers Guide, and then its sequel is the Cook's Guide. So in the first one, looking at how do you actually get to recognize and grow these foods either in your garden or if you're going to be a small scale grower, how would you grow them to sell them? Then the cook's guide went on to, and always getting feedback. So if then the growers would say, yeah, well if I'm going to grow these things, I actually want to know how you use them. I don't just want to be growing some niche thing, hoping there's going to be a market for this. I actually want to know what is this thing and what is it going to have, does it have meaning for me and my culture? And so that kind of request. So we did run some farmer trainer workshops here in 2023 in order to then start taking that training to farmers in order to encourage them beginning to plant and to start a kind of an alternative parallel local food system. So, you know, all of this foundational work of discovering the plants, knowing about how they grow and how you cook with them, there's been really great milestones of the level of interest and engagement that people want with this. And there's not enough of me to go around to do this work. And that's also why local wild needed to happen and why the community of practice needed to happen. Because it's. I mean, I think I need to sort of take 10 steps back here and explain a little bit about the Cape's foods. So this is a winter rainfall region and the foodways here were wild harvested foodways rather than that, there was a culture of cultivation of these foods. So hunting and gathering was the way in which these foods were accessed. And when the colonists arrived, they brought their plants and their agriculture with them. And it gradually sidelined, shifted to the side, both the people and the foods themselves, well out of the picture. And the. Whereas the rest of southern Africa, summer rainfall, predominantly those foods, there was a tradition and still is a tradition of cultivation. So the way in which you amplify what actually exists still in use may be slightly dormant, but there is knowledge of cultivation and knowledge of use that is still pretty active in the summer region for summer rainfall regions of Southern Africa. Whereas the Western Cape, if you go into very rural areas, you will still find some knowledge base, more of medicinal plants than of edible plants. But so really effectively the conversation about how this is working and how this is becoming embedded needs to be measured in quite a different way to the indigenous foods of the rest of South Africa, because these are very much more under researched and very much more forgotten. So there's a kind of like disparity in the level from which you're starting in order to amplify and embed. And so yeah, I mean, I don't know if that fully answers your question.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: So how would you actually define your main activities in, in making good? Especially when you say, you know, working with other people, especially communities or volunteers and bringing out this, what would be your main activities or how does a day look for you? Step by step in terms of planning activity or doing activity.

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: So my daily job at the moment, a lot of my focus has been on how do I find financial and research support for this work because it is significantly under researched. So a lot of my work is in network building, in attracting more and more people to engage with this work. My own so and that's really under the umbrella of the Local Wild Food Hub. My own personal making course journey is to continue to explore foods and to as I learn more and as I discover more, experiment with them. Because that's my makeup. I'm like into I love food, I love trying, I love nature. And so for me pushing my own boundaries to learn more and more is something that I will do till the day I die. In terms of where Local Wild is sitting in a kind of a trajectory, it's been an interesting journey of trying to figure out how does one embed the sort of structure of what needs to be done in order to really embed this in the food system very concretely. And one of the things that I've been working on recently is to develop project which starts to bring these various areas that in a way independently I've been working with separate people on. So in some instances I've been working with organizations to get small scale growers involved. And then I was working with private businesses to run workshops for chefs. And then I was working with a researcher at UCT working on knowledge ways in the Cederberg. And then I was working with on the community of practice and trying to attract all the various people together into one space to actually start framing, okay, what is our where are the commonalities? Where do we put our efforts? What is the goal of this collective in the community of practice in driving this vision to get indigenous foods back into our plates, back into our food system, back into our food landscapes so that collectively it takes it beyond my personal kind of vision. But it actually starts to include everybody else that might be doing various things. So it might be a researcher who's working on Honeybush in the Lungcliffe area or you know, so it's really trying to pull all these threads together and then to shape the strategy for how best to push this forward so that it really can shift people's lives, people's individual lives, so that these foods become reintegrated into our food systems and that they find themselves in our food landscape, so that it really brings wilders to people and planet. And my personal view on the sort of economy in which this needs to land is to approach it in an incredibly localized way. Because I feel that what the planet really needs is not this huge global economy that we have, which we know is actually in large part behind what is causing poverty and malnutrition and extensive planetary destruction. So my position is to say let's adopt a very localised approach. Let's get to know the foods that belong here, that are adapted to being here and that will grow easily here and that will bring biodiversity back into landscapes from which it's been lost. I mean our biodiversity in this country, 25% of our 10,000 plants in the Cape Floristic region are threatened. And in this Renosterfeld landscape in which we're sitting here, there's something like 2 to 4% of intact landscape left. So it's highly threatened, it's been completely overrun by western foods, western agricultural systems and the sort of intimate engagement with landscape that is going to make human beings better custodians of our planet. That is the journey that I'm trying to get back to and trying to do it starting with little people, starting with bringing nourishment through indigenous food to little people, starting with working with local communities and discovering what do they know and what is their interest and how much interest is there in engaging with us, working with the small scale growers, been working with farmer driven agrihubs for them to drive this forward. Lot of interest that's been emerging amongst them to engage with us. So there's a lot to be done and lots of people that need to be involved in it. And in a way local wild is the kind of hub that starts to just pull these things together. Because if we are going to shift this food system, we've got to do together, it can't. You know, a lot of what I've been doing has been individual little interventions and things all over the place. Which is of course course how it had to be. It couldn't be any other how, but it's kind of now the time where we need to bind this together and find how all of us collectively can contribute to making this localized food system shift. Really become quite embedded and doing the healing job it needs to do for People and the environment.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: Yeah. Ms. Libby, you said you didn't want to register it as a legal in jail structure. And so how do you, what would you say are some of your main funding sources or income streams to run your activities or do the type of work or at least besides your main funding streams or income sources, what is some of the major expenses also for you to do your work?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: Well, this is a very interesting question because you know, when you are embarking on what some people would call niche, but I don't want to call it niche because it's actually, actually centre but not yet recognized as a real need to explore, getting real innovation supported is incredibly difficult. I'm not an academic, I've never fallen under an academic institution. I chose to align myself with an organization because I'm also bridging academia and practice. So it's very much about being on the ground and making it happen. It's not just ideas, it's actually, let's actually make this happen. So my challenges for the first very, very many years was, was not having any money to support this. So I sold at market and I ran workshops and all. And then they reached a point where really I realized no, this is not, this is not the direction I should be going in. So effectively, until two and a half, three years ago, all my work has been unfunded and I've had incidental support when I offered workshops, let's say here at the Sustainability Institute there's a whole bunch of academics from all over the world interested in what I was doing. And so I'd get paid to run a workshop and other workshops, you know, so it's been, and I carried on doing my landscape design business for many years to supplement this thing, which for me was actually like a calling. So we had three years of funding and that landed this work here because I needed a conduit. I mean, I was also doing some work that was being supported, the research project up in the Cederberg that gave me some support for the three years while the NRF supported that research. So it's been cobbling together enough for me to keep pursuing this vision. And so now I'm in a position where again, we're needing to find a solid, you know, and the COE is great because that also enables us to carry on with that work. You know, we, we're in, in the next phase of, of Julian's support that he's achieved for the CoE to carry on with. We're embedded in that so we can carry on with building this network. And attracting more and more people to this. But in terms of our own work, it's been a real struggle to. And of course, you know, the funding for organizations is looking grim now because the whole global support landscape, philanthropy landscape is challenging at the moment. But you know, for me I have seen the, this work gain ground, get more rooted, more and more interest. It's become a stronger magnet. You know, I spoke about that idea where way back when I could hardly keep people's attention for very long because you know, one of the first things that people ask is oh, so what foods are these? And I have to say, well, you know, there's actually very little point in me telling you what they are because you really frankly wouldn't know them. So that's also why it's really great to have a garden here because then you can go and show people and you can give somebody a leaf and they can taste it and taste a meal from the Green Cafe and then it becomes real, you know. And then, I mean we had Renaissance SWAT's whole dietetic staff team here on Monday and you know, that was really cool to see how a field which they didn't even know existed when they drove in through the SI's front gate and suddenly like you could see a couple of them thinking, oh this is really cool. I think I want to get involved in some way in this. So you know, these are the sorts, this is the kind of stuff I keep doing because you'd never know where the support is going to land. And you, I just keep trying to spark it into life everywhere and anywhere. I mean you can see I'm just like, I'll talk till the cows come home. Trying to make people fall in love with this and see the value for our planet and for our country's people in doing this work.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: Ms. Luba, you did speak about this and you touched on it, but I need to ask again, what need or gap in the food system, especially in our food system in South Africa, does your initiative aim to address?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: So the fundamental one is the absence of local indigenous foods in any part of the food system. It's a little bit present in the really, really small scale Cape Flats food system there because I've been working for a long time but it's small and a lot of work still could be done to amplify that small scale system. That's kind of emergence. We know we quite well at one point and then the petty agrihub closed and we had to regroup but it's carrying on there. But it's the absence of these foods that really should be in our food system because potentially they can at an ecological level be restoring loss of biodiversity and healing our landscapes. So where the food system currently is completely embedded in destroying the planet's landscapes, bringing indigenous foods in is one of the pathways to addressing that problem. That the food system does not carry on destroying our local landscapes, food landscapes, environment. And then the other part of it is that at the moment there is still a very, very big separation between food traditions linked to the foods that are in our mainstream food system and the foods that got completely erased out of them. So of the summer rainfall foods you will see that sorghum, millet, you know, there's quite a lot of work that's going on to bring those into both the mainstream food system. You can get sorghum and woolies, millet and woolies these these days, but also small scale farmers making livelihoods out of them. So the summer rainfall stuff there, it's like it's going, it's going the winter rainfall, we're quite a bit behind it, but I think there's a very interesting opportunity that the winter rainfall foods give because it's, this is a biodiversity hotspot of plants that are phenomenally resilient and incredibly nutritious. We've done early research on their nutritional levels. They are way more nutritious than the counterparts that we have in the mainstream food system. And so that addressing how the mainstream food system is also keeping people malnourished and poor, that is another part of what bringing local indigenous foods into it is aiming to address. So that the nutritional levels of these foods can be helping, for example, in a feeding, in a feeding school, feeding project. But just in general that if people start to eat these locally adapted, very nutritious foods, we can also be addressing the other effect that the mainstream food system has in keeping people malnourished because all they can afford is empty calories, mass produced empty calories, really nuancing micronutrient, rich, local adapted foods and bringing them in. I would like to see into a networked small scale food system that benefits people on the ground. But I've deliberately not pitched straight away. Getting these grown at scale by people who already have land, already are well resourced. I want to see this being a groundswell that heals imbalances from the ground up.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: Ms. Ruby, you're very passionate and shows through your work. And so what would you say? What motivates your work? Or at least what, what values guides you?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: One of the Things that I find very triggering, which in a way I guess is probably an indicator of motivation, is injustice. So it was the primary reason why I switched from being a landscape designer to becoming involved with food because I thought what am I doing making privileged people's gardens nice When I live in a land beset by the kinds of issues that we have here. My passion for food needs to be directed into something that can make difference. So that for me is, and you'll hear me talk about healing. So for me that is the absolute driver is how can we actually change how food traditions are. And our landscapes are kind of held hostage in a globalized food system that really kind of makes it very, very difficult to make headway to shift it. It like there is resistance, like you know, small scale traders not helped much. You know, it's like it's a, it's a really tough and, and already, you know, financially it's so, so, so skewed. You know, the, the global system is well financed and powerful and is, has a strong relationship with government and we end up with our seed laws, you know, being seat saving being exchanged. It's like the strong power position there and this very under resourced very unjustly in, you know, inequitable scenario on the ground that is trying to surface and how one, you know, finding the ways in which these guys really start to support what's going on and you know, not just talk SDGs because nobody's managing to keep up with those really. That's not a system systemic shift that's going to come through putting those kinds of tick box targets in place. It's on the ground. It's on the ground that it's got to happen and we've got to find the people that are willing to support this because it's hard to come by.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: You speak a lot about pitching this work at the local level on the ground, embedding it with what we have here and not on a larger scale. So what would you say are the impact, what impact do you believe on others others in terms of communities, learners, your partners and the food system, what impact do you have?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: So a lot of the impact is seen. So one of the things that we've committed to in doing with the community of practice is to reach more into the community in this Lyndock Valley. So this Leyendoch Valley is a Renosterfeld landscape traditionally where it's winelands. So you'll see that indigenous landscapes got completely replaced by vineyards and by upmarket housing developments. And so finding the communities who Sit on the edges of this pretty big economy and interesting them in what would have been their roots. And actually the injustices that those people still see sit in. So the impact on having conversations about what it is I'm trying to achieve and kind of surfacing their realities, painful as it is, I have seen such moments of switch on that is painful because you have to acknowledge that as a, as a farm worker in this valley, your life switches still feels quite like we got slavery alive, you know. And to surface that which you have to surface before you start having the conversation about, okay, well, let's now start shifting this. How can we shift it through food? How can we shift it through what we grow? You know, at a personal level, seeing how people really value being seen, being heard and having something to start getting involved with, that, that is for me very, very meaningful. And this way in which we measure impact at scale, that is one of the things that I want to revisit so that like talking about numbers of people that come to a workshop yet yes, it has meaning because it has. But it has meaning because of what it shifts in people. So the shift is the kind of thing that I want to. And the number of people that shift and the number of people that want in that to me would be a measure of success. And then, for example, the one project we're hoping to land here is to develop a kind of a feeding ecosystem which plugs in growing, plugs in cooking, plugs in a supply system from, you know, that whole ecosystem, developing a model which is localized. So it includes getting to know this landscape and what are the foods that belong in this landscape and plugging those into that ecosystem. And that if you show how that's done, you can go and replicate that elsewhere. So it is totally different from destroying biodiversity and planting wheat by the thousands of kilometers. It's actually, no, no, let's restore relationship locally and replicate that so that we make good custodians and landscape impact in lots of different places that is locally responsible and locally involved. So in some ways it's more complex. But to me that is the route, that's the pathway to shifting humans relationships with food and food from places and really getting to know, you know, the way which thousands of years ago people would have eating, been eating what was on their doorstep. And then we had this green revolution and it went. And now my feeling is we've understood the damage that that has done to the landscape and to people's cultures. Let's bring it back and explore how can we do this at local levels and replicate it in. In places at local level. And we got tech on our side these days.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: You spoke about, you know, moving from CO to wildlife. So how is your initiative changed over time? What has remained constant and what, you know, what has changed over time? Because you started off with that, but then you moved over. So you know, what, how has it changed over time and what has remained the same? Or at least what you still hope to change.

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: So what has remained constant is my drive to keep exploring because there's so much that is not known. What has also remained constant is connecting with people one on one and opening relationships with people. That is absolutely. That will never change. Because to me, rooting it on the ground is a fundamentally kind of a foundational starting point. What has shifted for me personally over the years in my sort of direction, was to reevaluate what my role is in this. So, for example, when I was speaking about building a business for myself, I just said, whoa, whoa, whoa, that's not my place. And in fact, that should not be a lot of other people's places. So, for example, if a business comes to me and says, you know, we really want to start including Frameboss in our beer making process, the first question I'd say is, do you have a community project linked to this? And if the answer is no, I'd say, sorry, I can't, I can't work with you. Because to me it always, there has to be a community benefit. Otherwise just your commercial, you're commodifying something, which to me is completely the wrong approach. The other thing that has shifted is so a lot of what I started with exploring was because of where I had access. So my sister had a holiday house on the coast. And so a lot of the foods that I started exploring and that are covered in my two books are Sunfelt Food, Sandy Coastal Foods, and here we're in Rhein Osterfeld. So although we've got them planted in the garden here, it's really important. What has shifted is the need to understand this landscape. And so that has shaped how a kind of model for replication would be developed. Because it has to start with what belongs in these landscapes. How do we find them again? How do we learn about them? How do we discover what was being used? How do we make them again, interesting to use? And how do we, if we want to replicate this, how does one develop a kind of a framing that somebody who maybe isn't an academic figures out? How do you go about learning your own landscape? Because over and over, like when I've gone to farmer festivals and those sorts of things, somebody will get a dune spinach, oh, can I take this home and start growing this in Lesotho? And I say, well, no, actually not a good idea, but find its sister or brother that grows in Lesotho and grow that one. And so how do you support. Because conceptually, this idea of localizing food systems is attractive. It's not difficult to sell it as something that can make change happen for the good for our planet. But how you, how you put all the bits together together to support somebody who wants to do it so that they do it in a way that is going to be supporting local biodiversity and not just making the same mistakes that we made before and actually, you know, substituting incoming things into landscapes where we should be trying to benefit our own local biodiversities, wherever you happen to be. So there, there's been a shift in needing to find who to work with, because that is not my expertise. And so I've been working quite closely with a colleague at Kew with whom I'm running field trials, but also who, because of her position as a fellow at Kew, she has access to quite a lot of students and she's super smart. Nicola Kun. And so she's been getting some of her interns and a master students. And now we're pitching for a PhD that'll actually be. Get supervised from, from here to take this approach further. So how do you, how do you model distribution so that you can begin to say, okay, so here on the coast we've got dune spinach. But me personally, I know that if I go into the Cedarberg or even to the Northern Cape or even up the coast over there, there are other tetragonias that grow there. So let's figure out where they grow and let's see how we get that tetragonia being grown in that place, because that's locally responsible. So how do we, how do we. Is there a simple way in which an ordinary person can maybe start using freely available tools like Inaturalist, where people do record where plants are distributed? Or does one set up some kind of relationship with an institute that is happy to kind of do that work for, for people who are not able to do that for themselves? I mean, I'm projecting this, you know, into the future. But that has to be fundamental to this kind of work, because if you want to localize it, you need to help people with the tools to first of all discover what is actually local. And how do I decide which Ones I should, you know, it's what I had to do in the beginning. How do I decide which ones? I'm starting with those 22 plants in my book. How did I decide that I continue, how I used my own personal kind of inclusion exclusion criteria for doing it. But as we move forward, it's like we need to take it beyond Lumi making a decision about what to write about to actually understanding in a more researched knowledge base, you know, a knowledge based way how to approach this so that we do multiply it in a way that is going to have the impact that we envision.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: You spoke a lot about doing this for over many years and now you had to shift and adapt and change for this to work. So what would you say are some of the most challenges that you've encountered and how did you respond and adapt to these challenges that you've encountered along the way?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: So one of the ones of course is always financial. I mean, personally I've been more than happy to find ways to support this work which for the majority of the time, you know, I've had two and a half years of solid funding and that's it since 2010. The rest of it I've cobbled together bits of, you know, money for myself or involvement in projects, you know, that kind of thing. So that has been quite a big barrier. It's been a very, very big challenge. And now all over again is another challenge to find how do we support this work? In a way it's shifted a lot now because this has become a much stronger magnet. And it's as if when I started it I was like so bloody ahead of a curve that it was very difficult to get even an ear in. That has shifted globally. There's much more of a recognition of the value of indigenous knowledge systems and of the value of indigenous foods that are locally adapted and, you know, all of those sorts of things. There's now people want in, they don't necessarily know how. So in some ways we're in quite a strong position because we're a little bit ahead of that game as well. Should I stop for a bit? This is the end of school year. This is, this is part of what's wonderful about being here is that. Yeah, let's stop for a bit. You know, these, the children sounds are lovely. So one of the biggest challenges really has been finding financial support for this work. In the beginning, a lot of the time that I spent on this was supported by other things that I was doing to make money and then also by getting Sort of small projects going, like doing the research in the Cederberg and also by getting others to commit some of their time into doing this. So, for example, like getting Nicola at Kew involved and she has financial support through her institution to do some of this work that needs to be done, but really getting solid financial support to get this hub formalized and get a staff base. Because what's also amazing has been, for example, coming to the Sustainability Institute, so not registering an entity and therefore having to get swallowed up in the. All the business of running an entity that I parked. So that was a very conscious decision that I made. And it's still, I'm still parking it as a decision because if I do get financial support to formalize this hub, then it's already, then you're already in a better position where you can say, okay, fine, now I could maybe make this independent because now I've got some actual staff of our own that we can now make use of to push this further forward. So for now making use of the SI's resources and they've been incredibly generous in making their garden staff and getting their cooking teams on board with trying these things out and incorporating them into their meals with the funding that they've managed to achieve. So I've kind of like, I've embedded it in various ways, sort of springboarding off other, what other people already have going. But it really is the time now where not having our own dedicated team is really slowing things down. It's a real barrier to forward movement on our vision.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: We just previously, if you take a step back, you just spoke about how you needed to decide why this, why that, in terms of what you expect explore and what is written in your new book. So how do you make decisions about activities, partnerships, products or direction?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: In the early days when I sort of just embarked on this work, I've had really good people that have been interested in this who I've been able to call on, people like Scott Dremme, for example, who's at Stellenbosch University, and he's actually done quite a bit of work with Julian as well, you know, up in the Worcester area and so on with learning journeys and being able to. And so somebody who's very, very embedded in food systems work, being able to call on people with that kind of experience to say, sure, I'm not quite sure which way I should be going with this. And him just saying to me, just keep following your instinct on this, your instincts on well honed. And so that's in a way How I've had to, I mean there have been times when I've kept a huge number of balls in the air not knowing quite where's which one going to land. So lot of sort of keeping of options open in sums of parts that I had a sense of would eventually come together to contribute to a more holistic, integrated thing. But because I've always, you know, I grew up in the smallest beautiful kind of era where holistic thinking was my thing and it wasn't about being very, very over focused, I really grew up with this idea that this planet needs holism. My celial thinking was in my blood from when I was young. So instinctively anything that was going to contribute towards this thing, that is what I try to grow. And so a lot of it, a lot of my thinking was also quite reactive. So somewhere the call for funding would come by and then I'd say okay, how can I fit what I'm doing into that? Over the last few years I've become very much more intentional and strategic about how I think is going to be the best way to make impact. And that is a question of it just has to emerge and evolve and you can't force it down somebody's throat. You can talk your head off everywhere and hope somebody's gonna say hey that sounds cool, I want in, you know, and so that's the best I can do. And then it's now got to a sort of critical mass point where it now can start being quite actively massaged and hopefully made sexy enough for some funders somewhere to say, right, this is aligning with our vision as well. Let's support practice.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: What knowledge or skills or expertise are essential to do this type of work? Because I mean you don't some one day wake up and say you want to do this. So what would you say are some of the important knowledge or skills or expertise to, to do this work that you're doing, especially if you also have to educate somebody on it and tell them, you know, how this contributes to making, making the, your not just the food system sustainable but how can this contribute for you having your own plate of food on your, growing in your backyard and actually not having to go and buy things. Like you said, many of the things that you grew up with, grew up, that you grew up with was, you know, on your doorstep. So what type of knowledge and skills, you know, you send so for to carry to do this work?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: Some of the things, I mean it's such a cool question because I think one of the key character traits that I have, which I have found incredibly helpful over the years, is that I can kind of put myself into the shoes of another person. So whether you are a farm worker, you know, who's being threatened by the landowner to throw. Throw them off the farm, or whether I'm dealing with an academic, you know, or a government official, I somehow have the capacity to, like, get into their headspace and sell this idea to them. So that is, I think, the one thing that I'm sort of like, I can shapeshift in a way, too. Then I think the other thing, and what I'm speaking about now, is this role of being the sort of hub, of trying to pull these things together in order to make impact. So I'm not speaking about all the sort of individual little inputs that have to happen to build this hole, because there are lots of different ones. You know, you've got growers, you've got cooks, you've got researchers, you've got community act, you know, you've got lots of people that need to be involved in them, in this. And so then this other kind of notion of how do you bridge, how do you get a community person whose education level is very, very different to an academic, how do you get them to be having a conversation that is respectful towards each other and that leads to something useful for the people on the ground? Because I'm of the ilk of people who says academic needs to be at the service of useful on the ground stuff, not the other way around. So, you know, being able to bring those together, like, for example, in the community of practice, how do you hold a space where people don't necessarily see things the same way and you need to. And where you have a lot of quite difficult conversations quite a lot of the time, how do you hold that space safe? That. That's been one of the really important traits that I've had to work on, and being able to put myself in the shoes of another is definitely, and I don't even know, I think you can make it a practice, an active practice to learn it, but I think it's probably a natural inclination of mine, which is great, it's very useful. And then I think the other thing that you need is to be able to look big and dip into the detail. And that's a skill that I learned from our architecture and landscape architecture days, where as a designer, you're looking at the big environment, but then you're also detailing right down to the nuts and the bolts and the size of the piece of wood and, you know, how it's the order of putting it together and being able to talk to the workman on the, on, on the ground and what he's actually doing and ask him to redo stuff, but then also go into the boardroom and have a conversation with a quantity surveyor and the client and the angry client. And you know, that was also a really great, great place to cut my teeth on how do you work with completely different skills and sectors towards one end, completing a garden or completing a building to everybody's satisfaction. So that was quite a nice learning environment for me to figure out. How does, how does one work bringing a complexity of things together?

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: You spoke a lot. You spoke on the question that I want to ask now. You, I want to ask about, about your SWOT analysis. You know, your strengths, your weaknesses, your opportunities and your threats. And your strengths would be what internal strings help your work succeed. And you spoke about that now. And. But if you look at, you know, your SWOT analysis or SWOT analysis for yourself, like weaknesses, what internal limitations hold you back? Or opportunities, what external opportunities can you use to grow threats? What external risk or challenges do you face? So you spoke about strengths now, but what did you say? If you look at your SWOT analysis in terms of strengths, weaknesses, opportunities and threats, how does your work, what would you say? How does that look for you?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: So some of the risks have been. I wouldn't even want to call it a risk because that sucks. So failure focused, some of the things that have slowed things down, let's put it that way, is that so much just at the basic level of knowing which these plants are at the foundational level of people that you want to get involved with these, you know, bringing this work, say for example, here to the si, which has been an incredibly supportive environment, but getting the teams to know the foods, getting the teams to feel like they actually do want to experiment with them, how do you cook with them, you know, so it's at that level just even or like you go into a community situation and you discover how little people still know and how do you then start to even just bring the basic knowledge into, into the room from which you start to build. So that's been a really important thing to focus on. So it's like, yeah, that I spoke about not having the financial support more and more initially, the difficulty, because I've always known that there's a massive amount of research that needs to be done. So I've always focused on trying to get as much co knowledge development happening as possible right from on the ground level, getting farmers to actually start being the early adopters. And let's try this to finding supervisors for students. And the supervisor doesn't even supervise. Supervisor doesn't know these things. So I end up getting involved as a CO supervisor because I need to bring just at the basic level, you know, you need to be able to know what an ice plant and what a dune spinach looks like if you're going to research this. But that so, but that scenario has sort of begun to shift because over the years people have got to know more and more and you can, you know, people now know like June spinach. A lot of people when I do workshops or give a talk, people know June spinach now. And there's a restaurant down the road that's coming and buying it, you know, so the dial is shifting at the level of the amount that people know. But it's like this much in a much bigger, you know, 1700 others that remain to be discovered and explored and decisions to be made on are we going to try growing these or we're going to rather think of wild harvesting these. So it's like there's a whole bunch of base of knowledge that has to grow up and I've been working on it in this environment. But it means that wherever you go to next, you have to start by making sure that you grow an understanding of firstly what people's own food tradition is based on and how many of the things that belong around them are part of it. And how do you surface an interest to even engage on these? Because you don't want to be saying to people you must use these because they're local. You know that you need to get the buy in the agency to do it. So that is, those are some of the risks. Some of the food system risks also are. Is that I identified long ago is you get the early adopters, small scale growers to grow. Where's the market? I didn't have the, I didn't have the support capacity to push a marketing thing. I tried a bit through padi, they closed their doors and I kind of backpedaled a bit on that. And I thought, you know what, going to focus on the research side of things. I'm going to focus maybe also on getting the farmers to know these things. You know, which comes first? Do you get the end user to say I want them, I want them, grow them? Or do you get the small scale grower to early adopt and say to the growers, to the cooks, hey guys, I've got some of these, you know, so it's like this whole juggling act that you have to hope miraculously you can't pre plan at all, but you just keep putting these efforts in the places that you think is going to help them to come into a coordinated mycelium.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: You already spoke on this and you spoke a lot about it, but I want to ask also, what are some of the important stakeholders that you work with in if you can name them, name the important stakeholders and what roles do they play in terms of. Because you spoke about the coefficient. So what in terms of research and then is a sustainable institute. So what are some of the most name some of the most important stakeholders and what roles do they play in terms of lawmaking course work?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: So yeah, the COE definitely has been one of the very important ones because they had. They made it possible to spread interest much more widely. So that's been very significant. Their very first in commitment to it was in 2022 when we had a Greater Cape Heuristic Region Mapping workshop where we just started with, right, this is our vision, we want to surface these things, you know, who should be involved. You know, we started there and the CoE gave us some money just to run that work workshop and then that turned into a formalized partnership and they've been supporting us since then and they're taking us into their next sort of phase. So that's been fabulous in terms of really widening the stakeholder base. So I'm incredibly appreciative of that. Then the Sustainability Institute has been really, really great because this is an integrated environment in which to test lots of different things to test how do you create interest amongst people who don't know these things? How do you train trainers to get, you know, how do you get outside people in and having an interest? How do you make a child interested in eating a plate of food that has indigenous foods in it? So they're, I mean, I don't even want to call them a stakeholder. They're a partner. They're a partner that interests enables other stakeholders, be they outside people who come here and taste these things in the Green Cafe, be they, you know, it's the whole range of other stakeholders that get exposure to growing, to tasting, to researching, then others. Then Nicola Kun at Kew has been really quite an important. I wouldn't call her a stakeholder, I would call. Call her a partner because she has helped with pulling together early nutrition analysis which was a real barrier to getting Western Cape Department of Health involved. Pre Covid even they wanted in. But because we didn't have nutrition profiles of these foods, they said, well then you can't. So we worked together on that and we've done some analysis now as well. So first of all we try to understand where are the gaps, what has been analyzed, what has been. Even just of the 22 plants in my book, we're now wanting to expand that to understand more plants, to understand how do we make the decision about the next level of plants that we bring in beyond the 22. So she's been bringing people in, helping us with distribution, modeling and so on and so on. So she's been. And she spent a lot of time really getting to understand this landscape. So that's been found. Fantastic. Then my other stakeholder partner has been Rachel Weinberg at Environmental and geographical sciences at UCT. So with her I've been working since about 2016. We did three years of NRF project up in the Cederberg. And that has been. That was very fundamental in understanding rural stakeholders positions and understanding that peri urban and urban context. So peri urban being the. The winelands here and the urban context being Cape Flats and small scale growers there, which is where I spent a lot of my time working, getting a sense already in three different scenarios. Now what, what is the. Who are the stakeholders? What are the stakeholders? Relationships with these foods, interest in these foods. How do the, how do you, you know, how does the landscape relationship work in these different scenarios? So yeah, you know, they've been. That's been those sorts of stakeholders. The farmers have been really, really great stakeholders. The willingness within which they've taken this stuff on without there being any kind of a sense that there's going to be an understanding of there being a livelihood benefit. It was like somewhere at an identity level there's this wish to reclaim food identities that belong to this place. So those. And that goes beyond, you know, identity goes beyond any sense of money out of this. It's like who am I? Do I belong? Am I seed? Am I heard that is that part of my work with stakeholders is really a deep part of the healing journey.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: And I would assume also communities, especially.

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: Those communities, absolutely come to your workshop.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: Interested in it to learn.

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: They would also be absolutely that. So yes. So the, I mean the Cedarburg community is. Was also about learning, was learning workshops with the farmers here, workshops, sort of finding my way into these communities here. And also, I mean that's an amazing stakeholder as well. The Valley Collaborative, I mean they're actually a partner. So the Valley Collaborative is a partnership that, that they have with the Sustainability Institute. And they've been building relationships into the Liondock Valley with businesses, with organizations, with workers, with community kitchen people, so they've got an understanding of this landscape. So for local. Wow, that is an amazing opening into getting involved with a community. So those are the sorts of things which are really amazing partnerships to help this work forward. So that's like an opportunity that speaks to the opportunity. Again, the risk is that when you go in there, you don't know how it's going to land. You don't know if somebody's going to say, oh, what are you talking about? I've got far more important, you know, I'm going to find a uniform for my kid by January, you know, and now you want to get me involved in something which appears to be somewhat elite. You know, it's like, no, how do I root this in a place that has meaning and that shows that this is about being seen and finding a pathway to healing.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: What do you provide to your stakeholders, would you say? Value, service, knowledge, all of it.

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: Listening, ear, a different way of seeing.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: Explored 22 plants.

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: Yeah. And I mean, that's the tip of the iceberg. I mean, I had a difficult time deciding on those 22. It could have been very many more. Because, you know, you're also looking at, from the archaeological record right through to now, let's look into the future, you know, because we know that literally for millennia, the foods that belong here is all that was available for people to be flourished on. So I think the benefits that I bring is, I mean, it's limited because of not having a heck of a lot of money to give to this at this point in time. But more than that, what I've discovered is that the interest at community level is not so much about that. These are commodities, so it is not playing into the food system at that level. People who have been erased, ignored, marginalised, disconnected from their lineages. The value is of a light bulb going on and remembering and then being suddenly telling me about what, you know, they used to go and do in the veldt and the Karoo when they grew up. So that that is a value that is beyond money, that is at the core of justice and at the core of changing a fundamental injustice that still sits so strongly in this land.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: Which authorities or governing bodies do you have or do you work with in terms of, you know, local government, provincial government, national? Which governing bodies do you work with?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: So I mentioned Western Cap, Department of Health, Pre Covid, being interested in getting me to run workshops. But. But then when there wasn't nutrition information, they said, oh well, sorry. Well actually we can't go further with this. So I've recently reached out to the person that called on me then and I've seen that she's been following my work for years and you know, ever since. So that's going to be my port of call first port of call because it's in a way closest. They had this really great initiative called Western Cape on Wellness, which while. Which is very much an on the ground way of getting community understandings of how what people eat is influencing their health, what diabetes looks like, how you need to change your diet, how would the role that exercise play. So they were really positioning these kinds of hubs of engagement at community level. And so they wanted me to come and do indigenous food workshops in those kinds of rooted on the ground initiatives. So that's where I've. I've always tried to be grounded in what I do because I think that's where it really needs to to start. Policy level, I'm cynic, I'm cynical about how much difference policy really ever makes to what happens on the ground. I'm happy to work with other people who will do that work and I'm happy to, as time goes on, start to engage in that. So I also, I work internationally with lobbies like the agroecology movement that is growing stronger and stronger the world over. So I connect myself into those places because my work has been so thinly financially supported. I have sought out the places that will be supporting, not the places that are going to be confrontational or that I'm going to have to convince otherwise. I find the places that who will work with me, not against me, because why would I make my life more difficult than it already is?

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: You covered a little bit of this now, but what would you say are some of the, you know, do you have any global connections or collaboration beyond the local community?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: So. Q. Royal Botanical Gardens is one of them. I've had sort of secondhand connections with an organization in the States called People and Plants who are working on the ground with knowledge holders to sort of surface what they know. I mean, the organization that funded us for two and a half years are a Dutch philanthropy whose office was based in Kenya. So that was a connection. But. But other than that. And we're trying to get funding support internationally, but in terms of global partners at this stage, don't have them and hope to find ways to not necessarily open those doors individually as local wild but open those doors through partners that we work with here who have those relationships. So in that way, my thinking is always, always, how can I use the opportunities of my greater network where I've put a lot of my effort? I mean, that's also one of the character traits. I think that's really important in doing this kind of work is just make friends and build a massive network wherever you can so that you can leverage what other people are doing or other people's interest in what you're doing to realize something that on your own you've, you know, it's too big an obstacle.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: Thanks. I think. What is your vision for the future for your initiative, your community or the wider food system?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: Okay, so that's a three pronged question. My vision for the local Wild Food hub as an initiative is that we become resourced enough to have our own team to drive our vision forward. Forward so that we are not so dependent on other people's goodwill and resources. So that we can really be strong in driving this vision forward and that we have a really nice strong partner network around us. We've got our partner network already actually. So we're quite a. We're quite close to being able to. It's just money's in the way. And we also, you know, we're now in the position where there are people who would actually be interested in stepping into the kinds of roles that are needed. Because for a long time, if we'd got the money, we wouldn't necessarily have had the people who know enough or are interested enough to say, yeah, I'd love to come and work here. So we're at that place, which is great. And I think that I see that we will get there in terms of the wider community there. It's going to be probably driven by how many people we manage to pull in and where those people have a willingness or an interest to also work. Because we don't want to just start everything from zero. We want to amplify what people are already doing. So for example, here we have the Valley Collaborative. A huge amount of networking and relationship building, as always already happened here. To take that further is not going to be difficult. So in this local community, great. We can move forward if we get our ducks in a row. The same on the Cape Flats. I've been working for years there and they are very, you know, so there to push forward in that community is very much possible. Then we are also, we repeating our field trials in the Gritbos area. So. So there it's a very different Scenario where the work, in a way, is more ecologically driven because it's in the middle of a landscape where there are a lot of farmers that have given their land over to biodiversity protection. So there it's a way of seeing, answering kind of different, more environmental questions around bringing local foods into those landscapes that benefits the landscape, but also actually produces something that, that improves the landscape. So that's an environmental direction then the last one. So it was the wider food system. That is an interesting one because I think that a lot of that work.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: Because it cuts us on what you want to answer now, cuts us on how do you imagine the future of indigenous food system systems in Cape Town or places such as, you know, in. In the near future?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: So my vision for a food system is a localized one. So it really is. I mean, I can't see us. I'm not aiming to transform the global food system. I do hope to see the work that other people are doing to challenge the real disasters that are coming out of approaches to the global food system. That work I fully support. But my vision is to see localized, networked food system models that are embedded in local community identities and local environments. So that's what my vision for the foods. And it's not going to supply everything in the food system, but it is going to have a really important role to play in connecting people with place, connecting people with how to be custodians and look after the land and have a sense of identity and food identity as well that's connected to that because our planet is hungering for that and people are hungering for that. So that is a sort of a mycelial network that I see. Really, I would wish for it to not be in opposition to the bigger food system then actually that the food system, the global food system would man up and support these initiatives because they are not a threat at the level of, you know, the scale of the global economy, but they are potentially a massive contributor to improving things for people and for the planet. So that's, to me, that's my vision of this. This part of what I do can really help to heal. And really, we should find a way in which we all sit around the table and grow up and make things better for people on the ground and for the planet. I mean, come on already.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: How do global indigenous food debates, global indigenous food debates influence your local work?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: Very much. Very, very much global, but also here. So in the academic sphere, really getting my head around what decolonial research looks like and understanding how I've had to adjust not only my way of thinking, but also the words I use and the approach that you use in working in a decolonial way that I think is a very massive shift that is beginning to gain traction but still needs a lot more work. And it really makes a very, very enormous difference to how one works and how you question yourself. And for me in particular, as somebody whose lineage comes from Holland and more recently from Belgium, what is my role in. In this work? And what do I back away from and completely leave alone because it's there it goes, it doesn't need the likes of me. And where. Where is my skill set useful? Where can I. Where is it maybe easier for me to challenge an academic or whatever or a business person where a community person might really not have the tools or the courage or the agency to, you know, the conversation is so skewed. Bring me back to the question again.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: How do you. How do global debates affect.

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: So it is around what indigenous people's relationship with food and with landscape is. So whether you're talking about a chef who is of Inuit descent in America or whether you're talking about an Aboriginal person in Australia and they. And how they grapple with how indigenous ingredients are becoming elite there, or even now the sort of growing conversation around what Europe has lost connection with and their roots of being grounded in their landscapes and what they lost because there. There's also loss that's happened here. I think we have an even more complex situation because there's been so much Eurasia and there's been so much complexity that's been added into to our marginalized communities by incoming slaves from all sorts of places all over Africa and Asia that have brought their traditions with them, and rightly so. So we have to negotiate our way around apartheid politics, colonial politics, identities that are all over the place, people who really whose lineages are from here having very little left in the Western Cape of those lineage. And how do you make sense make of that incredibly complex situation and learn? You know, so definitely we. We all have to. We all learn from each other. The conversations are absolutely international. I mean, I haven't necessarily. Well, I have sit in the. Sat in the rooms at Slow Food where I've had, you know, been listened to panels where very old lineages from Japan and then somebody else, a Canadian person, you know, so. Or somebody from South America bringing their traditional way. So seriously, there's a lot of listening that needs to happen there because there's a huge amount of wisdom that at the moment is not getting heard and it's still being challenged. You know, the corporates are still, you know, pushing, pushing, trying to erase. I mean really, that's a belief.

entries.speaker: Loubie
entries.text: How, how would you like the indigenous food system to be positioned in the Western Cape or at least in South Africa, Especially when you look at our large household food and nutritional insecurity, the government not being able to, to address that gap and actually also telling people, you know what, you don't need to walk into a supermarket to go and buy food. You can grow some of your own things and make a port, of course. So how would you like the indigenous food system to be positioned not just in the Western, but inside of it?

entries.speaker: Keshia
entries.text: So I think perforce it has to be a multi pronged approach. I think any opportunity and every opportunity to influence what people put on their plates needs to be taken. Whether you end up with ice plant on the shelf at Woolies or whether somebody grows a pot of sea pumpkin in their backyard because that's the only soil that they can actually have. I think it has to be multi pronged. And I've adopted that approach all the way along to try and surface it wherever it wants to land initially, because you've got to want it and that's going to make a huge difference. So from people growing for themselves. I mean, for example, in all those years, you know, there I would be working in Khaylicha, I had a food garden there, 20, 16, 17, and I would walk on the pavements or go and park outside Isi Bivana and I would park on top of food that people didn't recognize as food. Got it. Now growing here, you know, it's like looks like a long stem broccoli, it's fantastically good food. And it's there just growing wild on the pavements of Khalilichi where people are hungry. And so it's at every level, I mean, where it can't only be one thing because yes, something like an ABCD approach, asset space, community development approach is one way in which we can change things to put the responsibility on disadvantaged people to sort their own problems out, you know, yes, to some extent let's actually also do that because you can have some agency over what you do and you can take responsibility for your own health.
